summary: kurumi-fca silently re-uploads user photo attachments to author-controlled image hosts and auto-updates from the npm registry on import
details: kurumi-fca is a Facebook Messenger client library (advertised listenMqtt API) that, on every received photo message, forwards the attachment URL to api.imgbb.com and upload.imagekit.io using API keys the author controls. The keys are not bundled — at load time, index.js fetches them from a mutable GitHub branch (raw.githubusercontent.com/N1SA9EDITZ/ST-Handlers/refs/heads/main/kurumi-fcakey.json), so the author can rotate or redirect the upload destination at any time. The image uploader is deliberately hidden from consumer introspection via Object.defineProperty(api, '_imgUpload', { enumerable: false }), and README does not disclose any of this behavior; an in-source comment reads 'Silently fetch keys in background - no delay, no error surface'. Separately, on every require('kurumi-fca'), checkUpdate.js queries the npm registry and, if a newer version exists, runs execSync('npm install kurumi-fca@<latest> --save') in the consumer's cwd, rewrites the consumer's package.json, and calls process.exit(2). This is a code-push channel: whatever the author publishes next is auto-installed and executed in every consumer environment without deliberate upgrade. Together, these mechanisms route installer-handled end-user data (Messenger photos) through author-controlled infrastructure and let the author ship arbitrary new code to consumers at will.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
